The **Penticton Vees** were a [junior ice hockey](/wiki/Junior_ice_hockey "Junior ice hockey") franchise in the [British Columbia Hockey League](/wiki/British_Columbia_Hockey_League "British Columbia Hockey League") (BCHL) based in [Penticton](/wiki/Penticton "Penticton"), [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"), Canada. As a charter member of the league, the Penticton franchise had been named, at different times, the Junior Vees (1961–1963), the Broncos (1964–1975), the Vees (1975–1979), the Knights (1979–1990), the Panthers (1990–2004) and the Vees (2004–2025). After the establishment of a [Western Hockey League](/wiki/Western_Hockey_League "Western Hockey League") franchise called the [Penticton Vees](/wiki/Penticton_Vees_(WHL) "Penticton Vees (WHL)") in 2025, the BCHL franchise was placed on hiatus while seeking to relocate.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Penticton_Vees_(BCHL)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The Junior Vees were one of the inaugural teams in the [Okanagan-Mainline Junior Hockey League](/wiki/British_Columbia_Hockey_League "British Columbia Hockey League") (OMJHL), launched in 1961. The league became the [British Columbia Junior Hockey League](/wiki/British_Columbia_Junior_Hockey_League "British Columbia Junior Hockey League") (BCJHL) in 1967.

The Penticton Vees were named for the Veteran, Valiant, and Vidette varieties of [peaches](/wiki/Peach "Peach") grown in the [Okanagan Valley](/wiki/Okanagan "Okanagan").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The Vees were [Mowat Cup](/wiki/Mowat_Cup "Mowat Cup") champions in 1968, 1973, 1980, 1981, 1982, 1985, as well as in 1986, when they were also [Centennial Cup](/wiki/Royal_Bank_Cup "Royal Bank Cup") champions. After 26 years since their last RBC Cup appearance, the Vees advanced to the championship game in the [2012 RBC Cup](/wiki/2012_Royal_Bank_Cup "2012 Royal Bank Cup") in [Humboldt, Saskatchewan](/wiki/Humboldt,_Saskatchewan "Humboldt, Saskatchewan"), defeating the [Woodstock Slammers](/wiki/Woodstock_Slammers "Woodstock Slammers") 4–3 on a goal by Joey Benik to win the Canadian National Junior A Championship.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In March 2025, the City of [Penticton](/wiki/Penticton "Penticton") was awarded an expansion franchise in the [Western Hockey League](/wiki/Western_Hockey_League "Western Hockey League"), which also was called the [Penticton Vees](/wiki/Penticton_Vees_(WHL) "Penticton Vees (WHL)"), and the BCHL franchise was placed on indefinite hiatus pending its relocation to another market.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Western Canada Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Penticton_Vees_(BCHL)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Western Canada Cup")]

[Western Canada Cup](/wiki/Western_Canada_Cup "Western Canada Cup") was the Western Canada Junior A Championship held from 2013 to 2017. The champions from the [AJHL](/wiki/Alberta_Junior_Hockey_League "Alberta Junior Hockey League"), [BCHL](/wiki/British_Columbia_Hockey_League "British Columbia Hockey League"), [MJHL](/wiki/Manitoba_Junior_Hockey_League "Manitoba Junior Hockey League"), [SJHL](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Junior_Hockey_League "Saskatchewan Junior Hockey League"), and a host team competed in [round-robin](/wiki/Round-robin_tournament "Round-robin tournament") tournament. After the round-robin, the first and second place team played for championship, the loser then played a runner-up g game against the winner of a third vs. fourth semifinal game. The champion and runners-up would then qualify to compete for the [RBC Cup](/wiki/RBC_Cup "RBC Cup") and the National Junior A Championship.

## National Junior A Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Penticton_Vees_(BCHL)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: National Junior A Championship")]

The [National Junior A Championship](/wiki/National_Junior_A_Championship "National Junior A Championship"), formerly known as the Royal Bank Cup from 1996 to 2018, is the annual championship tournament for [Hockey Canada](/wiki/Hockey_Canada "Hockey Canada")'s junior A hockey leagues. Depending on the year, various regional champions, qualifiers, and hosts participate in the championship tournament. The tournament usually consists of opening in a round-robin with the top four teams then advancing to a semifinal were the winners compete a championship game.

## NHL alumni

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Penticton_Vees_(BCHL)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: NHL alumni")]

In the [2023 NHL entry draft](/wiki/2023_NHL_entry_draft "2023 NHL entry draft"), [Bradly Nadeau](/wiki/Bradly_Nadeau "Bradly Nadeau") was drafted in the first round by the [Carolina Hurricanes](/wiki/Carolina_Hurricanes "Carolina Hurricanes") (# 30 overall) as the only first-round BCHL player selected and Aydar Suniev was selected #80 by [Calgary Flames](/wiki/Calgary_Flames "Calgary Flames"). 19 2022-23 players have committed to NCAA Hockey teams, including the two NHL draftees.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
